corpus.name: AurelianFlo Publisher Stack Demo Corpus
corpus.description: Sample premium content packaged for agent-buyable access.

articles.title: Agentic Commerce Primer
articles.summary: How wallet-native agents move from scraping pages to buying structured utility with x402.

sections.heading: Why direct agent payments matter
sections.markdown: Agents are no longer limited to scraping public HTML and hoping a human is on the other side of a lead form. With x402, an agent can request a resource, receive explicit payment instructions, pay from a wallet, and continue the workflow immediately.

sections.heading: What publishers actually sell
sections.markdown: The real commercial unit is not page access alone. Buyers want structured utility: markdown, JSON, corpus search, citations, and route-level analytics that tell them what to buy again.

sections.heading: Where AurelianFlo fits
sections.markdown: Cloudflare can gate content, but teams still need route packaging, wallet routing, search surfaces, and MCP-ready schemas. That is where AurelianFlo converts premium content into reusable agent products.

articles.title: Publisher MCP Blueprint
articles.summary: A design for turning content collections into search, retrieval, citation, and export tools that agents can actually call.

sections.heading: Tool surfaces, not page templates
sections.markdown: A publisher-grade MCP layer should expose content search, article fetch, citation extraction, and dataset export as explicit tools with clean input schemas. Agents need deterministic calls, not ambiguous prompts.

sections.heading: Wallet routing and commercial control
sections.markdown: A serious publisher surface needs route-level pricing, pay-to wallet routing, and clear examples for buyers. Without those pieces, a corpus is only partially monetized.

sections.heading: Operational requirements
sections.markdown: Teams need health probes, paid validation, and route analytics so they can trust that a premium corpus still works in production after the first announcement wave fades.

articles.title: EmDash Monetization Playbook
articles.summary: How to use EmDash x402 support as the access layer, then add productized content APIs above it.

sections.heading: Access versus utility
sections.markdown: EmDash makes paid access native by letting a site owner choose the content rule, set the price, and set the wallet address. That solves access. It does not solve search, extraction, or structured export.

sections.heading: Why productization wins
sections.markdown: The winning seller turns one article into multiple purchasable surfaces: article payload, markdown export, citation list, entities, topic bundle, and corpus search. Each route is a product, not a generic page fetch.

sections.heading: Practical rollout
sections.markdown: Start with ten routes under the publisher domain, validate them with paid probes, publish clear examples, and then expand into MCP tools once the commercial surface is stable.
